blurb: Madison Beer is playing the Roxy on July 1, which lands on a Wednesday but right at the start of a holiday week when a lot of people have already mentally clocked out. The Roxy is one of the better mid-size venues in Atlanta, good sound, good sight lines, and the Battery around it makes for a solid pre-show dinner. Beer has built a real following in the 20-something demographic and the Roxy crowd for this one will have energy. If you've got someone in your household who's been waiting for this tour to come through Atlanta, now is the time to actually buy the tickets instead of waiting and paying double on the secondary market.
scoring_notes: Madison Beer skews younger than the median Perimeter Post reader at 38, but the mix of young professionals in this audience means there's a real segment here. The Roxy is a great venue and the Battery location adds value. Scored lower on demographic fit because the artist's core audience is late teens to mid-20s, which is on the younger end of this newsletter's readership. Still worth including as a pick for the younger professional segment.

blurb: Sewell Mill's MiniCon is back for 2026 and it's exactly what it sounds like: one day, every fandom, no gatekeeping. Games, vendors, food trucks, and workshops fill the cultural center from 10 to 5, and the vibe skews fun and accessible over intimidating and niche. If you've got kids who are into gaming, anime, comics, or really anything with a fan base, this is a Saturday well spent. And honestly, if you're an adult who grew up on any of this stuff, there's no shame in showing up solo either. It's free, it's local, and it's the kind of day that's hard to have a bad time at.
scoring_notes: The young families and younger end of the professional demographic in this audience will find this genuinely useful. It's free, locally produced, and the all-ages format gives it broad reach. The uniqueness score is moderate because fan conventions exist in various forms around Atlanta, but a free, neighborhood-scale version at Sewell Mill is more accessible and less overwhelming than Dragon Con. Good Saturday option.

event_name: Roswell Music Club: Cameron King, Tenor
blurb: Cameron King is one of classical music's most talked-about young tenors right now, and he's performing at Roswell River Landing on Sunday the 21st as part of the Roswell Music Club series. He trained at Indiana University's Jacobs School of Music and has been racking up performance credits that put him well beyond the local circuit. The River Landing is a beautiful outdoor setting on the Chattahoochee, which makes this the rare classical music event that doesn't require a dress code or a serious commitment to the art form. It's a Sunday, the setting does half the work for you, and you'll walk away having heard something genuinely impressive. Keep an eye on the Roswell Music Club page for time and ticket details.
scoring_notes: The highly educated, higher-income Perimeter audience has real overlap with classical and live music fans who want something more elevated than a cover band. The River Landing outdoor setting softens the barrier to entry for people who aren't die-hard classical listeners. Cameron King as a rising star adds a discovery angle, meaning this audience gets to say they saw him early. Time not confirmed is a flag, but the event itself is real and the fit is solid.

event_name: Atlanta Braves vs. St. Louis Cardinals
blurb: The Cardinals are in town for a series at Truist Park, and June 30 is a Tuesday night that actually has a good reason to get off the couch. Truist is one of the better ballpark experiences in the country, the Battery makes the whole area worth showing up early for, and a midweek Braves game in late June hits differently than a packed weekend series. Tickets are easy to find, you won't be fighting half of Atlanta for a parking spot, and the weather in the evenings has finally gotten to a point where sitting outside for three hours isn't a commitment. Grab a group, get there by 6, and do dinner at the Battery before first pitch.
scoring_notes: Braves games are a staple for this audience demographic and Truist Park is close enough to feel local. The Cardinals are a genuine rivalry matchup with national fan base crossover, which adds a small bump to the uniqueness score. Midweek timing is a mild disadvantage but also means better parking, better access, and a more relaxed crowd, which this audience tends to appreciate. The Battery dining scene makes it a full evening rather than just a game.

event_name: National Typewriter Day with Typewriter Tom
venue: Mimms Museum of Technology and Art, Roswell
blurb: National Typewriter Day is June 20, and Typewriter Tom is bringing his collection of iconic machines back to the Mimms Museum for an afternoon called "The Machines That Wrote America." This is not your average Saturday museum visit. Tom has done this before and the collection is genuinely impressive, and the story of how typewriters shaped journalism, literature, and American culture is more interesting than you'd expect. If you've got a soft spot for analog things, a curious kid who has never seen one up close, or a parent who actually used these machines at work, this is a low-key great afternoon. It's in Roswell, it runs until 5, and it's the one thing on your weekend agenda that nobody else will have done.
scoring_notes: The 70% college-educated audience skews toward cultural and intellectually interesting events, which is exactly what this is. The Mimms Museum is small and under the radar, which makes it feel like a genuine discovery rather than a programmed outing. Uniqueness score is high because there is genuinely nowhere else in the region doing this on this date. Family-friendly but also works for the empty nester segment that wants something different on a Saturday afternoon.

event_name: Argentina vs. Austria World Cup Watch Party at Campus 244
venue: The Lawn at Campus 244, 244 Perimeter Center Pkwy NE, Dunwoody
blurb: Argentina, the defending World Cup champions, play Austria on Monday, and Campus 244 is putting a free watch party on the lawn for it. No tickets, no reservation, just show up and watch Messi and company do their thing in the middle of your lunch hour. The Perimeter Center location makes this genuinely convenient if you're working nearby or just want a midday excuse to be outside. It's casual, it's free, and Argentina vs. Austria is not a throwaway group stage match. If you've been looking for an easy way to plug into the World Cup energy this summer without committing to a full night out, this is it.
scoring_notes: Campus 244 sits directly in the Perimeter audience's backyard, which is a strong geographic fit. Free entry, outdoor setting, and a marquee World Cup matchup make this easy to recommend. The young professional segment of this audience is very likely to follow international soccer. Midday Monday timing is the one soft spot but it's summer, schedules are looser, and the convenience of the location offsets it.

event_name: World Cup Weekend Watch Party at Zanzibar ATL
venue: Zanzibar ATL, 350 Northridge Rd, Sandy Springs
blurb: Atlanta is one of the host cities for the 2026 World Cup, and the energy around it this summer is real. Zanzibar is leaning all the way in with an elevated watch party experience that pairs live soccer with brunch vibes, after-party energy, and the kind of crowd that actually knows what they're watching. This isn't a bar with a game on in the background. It's a full production, and it lands on June 19, which means you've got a Friday night and a reason to go out already built in. Sandy Springs is right in your backyard, so there's no excuse not to check it out. Get there, find a seat, and enjoy the fact that the world just showed up in your city.
scoring_notes: The 2026 World Cup is a once-every-four-years event and Atlanta hosting makes local watch parties genuinely special rather than just routine bar sports nights. The Zanzibar venue skews adult and upscale, which matches the young professional and empty nester mix here. Friday evening timing is ideal. The $105K median income audience won't blink at a cover or drink minimums.

blurb:
Four hours of crowd-requested, audience-driven live music at Park Bench inside The Battery, and no cover to get in. Dueling Pianos is one of those shows that sounds like a gimmick until you're actually in the room, and then you're the loudest person there.

Two pianists take requests, play off each other, and feed off whatever energy the crowd is bringing. It's participatory without being forced, which makes it a great pick for a Saturday night when you want to go out but don't want to sit through a formal show. Tables fill up fast, so reserve one before you show up expecting to walk in and grab a good seat. Grab dinner somewhere at The Battery first and make it a full evening. For the East Cobb crowd who wants a Saturday night that doesn't require driving into the city or buying expensive tickets, this one's sitting right in your backyard.
scoring_notes: No-cover format is accessible and appealing even at $118K median income because it removes friction, not because they can't afford it. The Dueling Pianos format is genuinely entertaining for the 40s demographic. Slight uniqueness penalty because it's a recurring show, but the June 27 date is specific and the Battery location is East Cobb-adjacent.

blurb:
Sunday afternoon baseball at Truist Park, and the kids go home with a Braves LED Light giveaway. That's a pretty solid pitch for a Father's Day weekend follow-up.

The Braves are hosting Milwaukee on Sunday the 21st at 1:35pm, which is about as easy a summer Sunday plan as you'll find. Day game, no traffic drama getting home before dark, and a giveaway item that actually gets used after the game. If you've got teens in the house who need something to do before the week hits, or you're just looking for a reason to get outside and eat a hot dog in the sun, this is the one. Truist Park is basically East Cobb's stadium at this point. Get the tickets before the good seats in the shade disappear.
scoring_notes: Regular season game is not uniquely compelling on its own, but the day game format, kid giveaway, and Sunday timing all serve the East Cobb family demographic well. Lower uniqueness score reflects that this is a standard game, but the audience match is strong.

event_name: Atlanta Braves vs New York Mets
blurb:
Braves versus the Mets on July 3rd at 7:15pm, with the full summer evening experience at Truist Park and a holiday weekend Friday to make it feel like a real occasion. The Mets have become one of the better rivalries in the NL, which means the crowd will have some actual energy in it.

A night game on the eve of a holiday weekend is one of those plans that always sounds better out loud than on paper, and then you get there and wonder why you don't do it more often. Get the tickets, grab dinner at The Battery before first pitch, and let the game take you through to 10pm. If you've got older kids who want a reason to stay out late or you're just looking for the quintessential summer Friday, this is it. Holiday weekend means schedule flexibility. Use it.
scoring_notes: Strong holiday weekend timing and Mets rivalry adds more energy than a typical game. Night game on July 3rd with The Battery dining options nearby is a complete evening plan that fits the East Cobb demographic well. Slightly lower uniqueness because it's still a regular season game, but the July 3rd context elevates it.

event_name: Black Experience Tour at Barrington Hall
blurb:
Barrington Hall is one of Roswell's most recognizable historic homes, but most people who've driven past it have never heard the full story of who actually built and maintained it. This tour does that.

The Black Experience Tour focuses specifically on the lives of the enslaved people who lived and worked at Barrington Hall, and it's a one-hour guided experience that gives real historical context to a place most East Cobb residents think they already know. For parents who want to give their kids something more substantive than a summer of screens, or for anyone curious about the actual human history underneath the pretty Greek Revival architecture, this is a worthwhile Friday afternoon. It's in your backyard, it's educational without being a lecture, and it's the kind of thing you'll still be thinking about over dinner.
scoring_notes: Roswell location is highly local and relevant for East Cobb readers. The 65% college-educated audience skews toward substantive cultural experiences. The midday Friday time slot is a mild limitation for working households, but summer schedules open that up. Uniqueness score reflects that this is a specific, meaningful experience that differs from standard historic home tours.

blurb:
You get your own bar station, your own tools, and a lead mixologist walking you through three cocktails made with ASW's award-winning spirits. That's the setup for this monthly cocktail class at ASW Distillery inside The Battery, and it's a genuinely fun 90 minutes that doesn't require you to know anything going in.

This is the perfect Sunday afternoon activity for a couple who's tired of brunch and wants to actually do something. You're hands-on the whole time, building real cocktails from scratch, and you leave with the knowledge to recreate them at home. ASW makes serious spirits, the Battery location is easy to get to, and Sunday at 2pm means you're home before dinner with a good story and a slight buzz. For the East Cobb crowd that appreciates quality over novelty, this one quietly overdelivers.
scoring_notes: Adult-skewed experience that fits the empty nester and established couple demographic well. $118K median income means the class fee is comfortable. Monthly format means it recurs, but the June 28 date is specific and actionable. Good fit for readers who want something interactive and social but not a huge production.

blurb:
Forty-five years. That's how long Roswell has been throwing this patriotic concert series, and it hasn't gotten old because it keeps drawing people who actually care about a real community celebration rather than just the fireworks at the end.

The June 26th concert is right in Historic Roswell, which means you're already in one of the best settings in the metro for an outdoor evening. Bring a blanket, grab food nearby, and settle in for a night of patriotic music under the summer sky a full week ahead of the July 4th crowds. If you've got older kids or a spouse who's been asking what you're doing this summer, this is an easy answer. Free, close to home, and 45 years of proof that it's worth showing up.

Don't overthink it. This one practically plans itself.
scoring_notes: Roswell is East Cobb's backyard. The 45th anniversary gives it legitimate milestone status. Free admission is a nice bonus (though not the selling point). Mixed family and empty nester crowd both fit the event format well. Pre-July 4th timing makes it feel timely and relevant.

event_name: Bourbon & Brisket Festival at The Battery Atlanta
venue: The Plaza at Battery Atlanta, Atlanta
blurb:
Eight hours of premium bourbon, slow-smoked BBQ, live music, and games on the plaza right next to Truist Park. That's the pitch for the 3rd Annual Bourbon & Brisket Festival, and honestly it doesn't need much more than that.

You're looking at a full Saturday afternoon and evening format, the kind where you show up at noon and realize it's 6pm and nobody's complaining. Multiple bars, pop-up bourbon tastings from premium brands, serious BBQ, DJs, and enough activity that you can roam around rather than park yourself at one spot all day. It's built for adults who want something more than a backyard cookout but don't want a ticketed seated experience either.

For a household that's used to spending real money on a good night out, this one delivers. Get your tickets before the crowd figures out that a Saturday like this at The Battery in late June is basically the definition of a great summer day.
scoring_notes: Strong fit for the East Cobb demographic. $118K median income means the ticket price is no concern, and the adult-skewed bourbon and BBQ format plays perfectly to the 42 median age. Not a once-in-a-lifetime event but it's annual and it sells, and June 27 timing is prime summer Saturday territory.

blurb:
This isn't your standard Braves game experience. Bats & Bites gives you early access to Truist Park, a guided tour of the ballpark, a meet-and-greet with a former Braves player, and a food sampling session in the Outfield Market featuring the newest items on the stadium menu. Then you watch batting practice from the outfield.

For the Braves household that's been to a hundred games and knows the drill, this one flips the script. You're getting behind-the-scenes access that the average ticket holder doesn't see, plus a legitimate reason to be at the park on July 3rd before the Mets series opener that night. If you've got a baseball-obsessed teen or a spouse who would genuinely geek out over meeting a former player on the field, this is the move. July 3rd is a holiday weekend Friday, so the timing couldn't be better to kick off the long weekend early and do something that actually feels special.
scoring_notes: Uniqueness score is high because this is not a standard game ticket. Behind-the-scenes ballpark access, former player interaction, and food sampling is a differentiated experience. July 3rd timing on a holiday weekend Friday is a strong practical hook. Braves fandom runs deep in East Cobb.

event_name: Father's Day Brunch at C. Ellet's Steakhouse
blurb: Father's Day lands on a Braves game day this year, and C. Ellet's is leaning all the way into it. Show up Sunday morning for a jazz brunch with ribeye, filet, mimosas for the table, and a live band setting the mood before the 1:35 PM first pitch. It's the kind of meal that actually feels like a gift, not just a reservation someone made out of obligation. You eat well, you hear some music, then you walk across to the ballpark. If your family is still figuring out what to do for Dad on Sunday, this is your answer.
scoring_notes: Father's Day is a high-relevance hook for this audience of established families and empty nesters. The combination of jazz brunch plus same-day Braves game is genuinely unique and time-sensitive. Steakhouse-level pricing is a non-issue at $118K median household income. Strong emotional hook.
